Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

REDUCING THE NUMBER OF NEURONS OF DEEP RELU NETWORKS BASED ON THE CURRENT THEORY OF REGULARIZATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We introduce a new Reduction Algorithm which makes use of the properties of ReLU neurons to reduce significantly the number of neurons in a trained Deep Neural Network. This algorithm is based on the recent theory of implicit and explicit regularization in Deep ReLU Networks from (Maennel et al, 2018) and the authors.

We discuss two experiments which illustrate the efficiency of the algorithm to reduce the number of neurons significantly with provably almost no change of the learned function within the training data (and therefore almost no loss in accuracy).

## 1 INTRODUCTION

## 1.1 MOTIVATION

In this work, we investigate a particular type of deep neural network. Its architecture (see section 2) can be better understood, thanks to the previous work on wide shallow neural networks: Neyshabur et al. (2014); Ongie et al. (2019); Savarese et al. (2019); Williams et al. (2019); Maennel et al. (2018); Heiss et al. (2019) and unpublished work of the authors on deep neural networks (with arbitrarily many inputs and outputs).

These results state that _ℓ_ 2 weight regularization on parameter space is equivalent to _L_ 1-typed P- functionals on function space under certain conditions. This implies that the optimal function could also be represented by finitely many neurons (Rosset et al., 2007).

With the knowledge of these properties, we were able to design a reduction algorithm which can reduce infinitely wide (in practice: arbitrarily wide) layers in our architecture to much smaller layers. This allows us to reduce the number of neurons by 90% to 99% without introducing sparsity (which allows more efficient GPU-implementation (Gale et al., 2020)) and with almost no loss in accuracy.
This can be of interest for deploying neural networks on small devices or for making predictions which are computationally less costly and less energy consuming.

## 1.2 LITERATURE / LINK TO OTHER RESEARCH

Many papers have been written on the subject of reducing neural networks. There is the approach of weight pruning, by removing the least salient weights (LeCun et al., 1990; Hassibi & Stork, 1993; Han et al., 2015; Tanaka et al., 2020). A different technique is pruning neurons Mariet & Sra (2015); He et al. (2014); Srinivas & Babu (2015), which does not introduce sparsity in the network by removing single weights, but reduces the number of neurons. For CNNs there are ways to prune the filters (Li et al., 2016). In transfer learning, one can prune the weights with decreasing magnitude (Sanh et al., 2020). All these techniques require the same steps: train a large network, prune and update remaining weights or neurons, retrain. And for too much pruning, the accuracy of the pruned models drops significantly, also it might not always be useful to fine-tune the pruned models (Liu et al., 2018).

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Another approach is knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Ba & Caruana, 2014) where one establishes a teacher/student relation between a complex and simpler network.

The lottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle & Carbin, 2018) states that ”a randomly-initialized, dense neural network contains a subnetwork that is initialized such that—when trained in isolation—it can match the test accuracy of the original network after training for at most the same number of iterations”.

In this work, the method can be related to neuron pruning, in that we are working directly on a large already-trained network. We are, however, trying to preserve the learned function contrary to the cited techniques which focus on the loss function and where pruning results in a different learned function. Therefore, in our algorithm, the neurons are not only pruned but rather condensed, put together into new neurons which contain all the information learned during training. Our method, hence, does not require retraining. But it is beneficial to further retrain the network, and reduce it again, in an iterative process.

## 2 DESCRIPTION OF THE ARCHITECTURE

Starting from a traditional Shallow Neural Network with ReLU activation function (see fig. 1) (which contains a single hidden layer) and is _ℓ_ 2 regularized, we will define two variants of a _One Stack Network_ . First, by adding a direct (or skip) connection between the input layer and the output layer (see fig. 2), one can obtain the simplified One Stack Network.

Second, by adding a layer in the middle of this direct (skip) connection, one can get a One Stack Network (see fig. 3). This new layer contains neurons with a linear activation function (the identity function multiplied by a constant). It contains as many neurons as the minimum between the number of neurons in the input layer and the number of neurons in the output layer, it also has no bias.

Furthermore, we call it the _affine layer_ and the new weights before and after it the _affine weights_ . These new weights can also be _ℓ_ 2 regularized, but typically by a different hyperparameter than the non-linear weights.

The architecture that we are going to study can be described as a sequence of stacks, or a _Deep Stack Network_ . We repeat the pattern described above (see figs. 4 and 5). Since the output layer is at the end of the architecture, we call all intermediate layers related to the output layers as introduced earlier (typically containing few neurons _dj_ ): _bottlenecks_ . The bottlenecks contain neurons with a linear (identity) activation function. For every stack, all parameters are regularized except for the biases in the bottleneck.

_Remark_ 2.1 _._ (ordinary fully connected deep neural network) Note that our algorithm could also be applied to an ordinary fully connected deep ReLU neural network and would output a neural network with skip connections. But if every second layer is much wider ( _dj ≪ nj_ ), we have a much better theoretical understanding of what is happening and typically we can get better test accuracy with fewer neurons. We don’t use ReLUs on the bottlenecks for easier interpretation. The compression-rate would not suffer if we apply our algorithm on architectures without affine weights.

## 4 REDUCTION ALGORITHM

We will now consider a one-stack network according to the description of the architecture given above (see fig. 3). Therefore, we can fix _j_ and write _W_ := _W_[(] _[j]_[)] for example in this case. Then, the reduction can be applied similarly on every stack.

## 4.1 REMOVING AND REPLACING OUTSIDE NEURONS

The main idea of this step is to combine the information of all the non-linear (ReLU) neurons whose kink positions are outside the convex hull of the previous stack’s representation of the training data

We define the _outside neurons_ as those neurons that will always return a zero or always return strictly positive numbers for all training data points. Therefore we find two cases. Either the activation function of the outside neuron acts as a zero function in which case we can simply remove it from the ReLU layer as it makes no contribution to the learned function, or the activation function of the outside neuron acts as an identity function. In the latter case, the contribution of the neuron to the learned function is affine and we can add it to the affine weights and remove it from the ReLU layer. In order to do this, we will define _W_ and _c_ :

where _M_ is the set of outside neurons whose activation function is positive for all training data points. Note that we can remove the neurons whose activation function acts as a zero function without any replacement since they do not contribute to the learned function. Note as well that _wk_ are column vectors and _vk_ are row vectors and their multiplication in that order gives a matrix. Therefore, _W_ affine is the result of a sum of matrices and _c_ affine is a vector, both summarize the contributions of all outside neurons to the learned function.[5]
We are now finally able to remove the outside neurons and add their contributions to the affine weights. The question we ask ourselves now is: how do we determine which neuron is an outside neuron?

We decided to loop through every ReLU neuron, then check their prediction for every training data point. If the prediction was always zero or always strictly positive, we could be sure that we found an outside neuron. Of course such a method is also computationally expensive, and would hence be very impractical if we had many neurons and many training data points. But we observed empirically that it was not necessary to loop through _every_ training data point (more on this in the experimentation chapter).

_Remark_ 4.1 _._ Exactly speaking, the learned function will not be the same as before. In fact, it will be different outside of the convex hull of the training data. But these changes further smoothen the extrapolation behavior of the learned function without affecting training accuracy. Removing the outside neurons does not change the function on the training data and it does not change the stack’s function inside the convex hull of the previous stack’s representation of the training data.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We performed two experiments. First, a one-dimensional easy to visualize example. Second, we train the architecture on the MNIST dataset (handwritten digit recognition).

## 5.1 COMPOSITE SINUS

At first, we perform a simple experiment on artificially-generated data[6] . We create a map _f_ : R _→_ R[7] : _x �→ f_ ( _x_ ) := ( _fi_ (sin 3 _πx_ )) _i∈{_ 1 _,...,_ 7 _}_ from a one-dimensional input to a multi-dimensional output. All the outputs are functions of the sine of the (scaled) input.

We choose an architecture with three stacks (see fig. 5), and _dj_ = 1 neuron in each bottleneck. We train the network at first without regularization, and then with some regularization. The plots are given before and after reduction in figs. 6 and 7. The green curves show the function learned by the stacks themselves, while the red curves show the composition of the function learned by the network _up to_ that stack. ## 5.2 MNIST

The last one-dimensional example was hopefully very intuitive and visual, yet it would now be interesting to test the architecture on a higher-dimensional dataset. We chose to apply it to MNIST, the very popular handwritten digit recognition dataset.

In fig. 8 we visualize the comparison between our Reduction Algorithm and the standard pruning method as implemented by Tensorflow (neither the reduced model nor the pruned model were retrained). The first two plots (training and testing) compare the two methods for ten separate models which were trained then automatically reduced (given different drop in accuracy thresholds between 10 _[−]_[8] and 10 _[−]_[2] ) and pruned for various compression rates. The training scheme was always the same: trained slightly with no regularization and then trained with increasing regularization, decreasing learning-rate, and increasing batch-size using an adam optimizer. We chose three stacks with _nj_ = 1024 neurons in the ReLU layer and _dj_ = 16 neurons in the bottleneck layer per stack.

We observe that with no loss in accuracy (10 _[−]_[8] ) we achieve a compression factor of at least 9 for each seed (typically more than 12). This fits nicely to the theory, that the learned function equation 1 needs a finite number of neurons _n[∗] j_[(independent][of][the][original] _[n][j]_[as][will][be][also] discussed in the next paragraph) and all other neurons are redundant. Thus, fig. 8 suggest that the theoretical _n[∗] j_[is probably below][ 100][ in this scenario.][If we reduce the network to the suggested] _[ n][∗] j_[,] our algorithm would misclassify typically around 100 times less images of the training set than the standard pruning for the same compression factor and the same seed. We can also see that if we require the train or test misclassification to be below any threshold less than 1% respectively 2% our method clearly outperforms the standard pruning method for every seed.

Figure 9 also shows two plots which compare the Reduction Algorithm and the pruning method, for different number of neurons _nj_ in the ReLU layer of every stack. As one can easily see, the compression factor goes up as the number of neurons increases (per stack, for three stack and _dj_ = 16 neurons in the bottleneck layer) and the plots help visualize that we can indeed reduce the networks to a rather constant number of neurons _n[∗] j[≤]_[100][per][stack][(no][matter][how][many][neurons] _[n][j]_[we] use), which illustrate the result of the theory in section 3 about a finite number of neurons needed to represent the learned function.

(1) for the theory in section 3 to hold), indeed the clear trend is that the compression factor increases as the training time increases.

## 6 CONCLUSION AND FURTHER WORK

In this paper, we have introduced a neural network architecture which allows to be reduced significantly in the sense that we can remove or replace many of its ReLU neurons by fewer ReLU neurons. This property alone is already of interest for a number of reasons evoked in the introductory section. We could also analyze the learned function of each stack and observe the composition of functions.
Inspired by the lottery ticket hypothesis, we could argue that it would be difficult to train a smaller neural network to generalize as well as the reduced model (when both have the same dimension).

Worth noting is that if one tries to evaluate an upper bound of the generalization gap (difference between the empirical error and true error) based on the sum of the weights, (Kawaguchi et al., 2017), one could potentially find a better estimation by taking the sum of the weights after reduction.

Also of interest is that, after the reduction algorithm was applied, we obtain a fully-connected neural network. Therefore this method of reduction/compression could be further improved by applying for example weight pruning on top of it.

There are many possibilities for future work, and many are going to be explored very soon. We want to investigate every step of the Reduction Algorithm in more detail whether it is for checking fewer training data points by choosing them non-randomly in the first step, or finding a better and more efficient way to cluster the remaining neurons in the last step.

Similar ideas (of reducing the number of neurons) could potentially be applied to the bottleneck layer (at least the second and third step of the algorithm, since they do not require the presence of affine weights). Investigating the performance of the algorithm on different datasets and comparing it to other neural network architectures is certainly of interest as well as extending it to be used in CNNs and RNNs.
Since our algorithm is highly theory driven, we might use these ideas to better theoretically explain the lottery ticket hypothesis (Frankle & Carbin, 2018).

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and potentially high-impact problem: compressing over-parameterized ReLU networks by "condensing" neurons rather than relying on traditional pruning, which avoids the need for extensive retraining. The motivation is clear, and the connection to $L_1$-type P-functionals provides a robust mathematical foundation that distinguishes this work from heuristic pruning methods. However, the submission currently suffers from significant clarity and structural issues. The reliance on "unpublished work of the authors" to justify key theoretical claims significantly weakens the paper's self-contained nature and credibility, and the transition from the problem statement to the proposed "Deep Stack Network" architecture feels abrupt. The manuscript would benefit from a more rigorous formalization of the reduction algorithm itself—which is currently only vaguely described—as well as a clearer distinction between the specific architecture proposed and standard deep learning models. As it stands, the paper feels like a preliminary report rather than a complete, rigorous submission suitable for a top-tier venue, as critical mathematical definitions and implementation details are missing.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'outside neurons'
comments.quote: We define the outside neurons as those neurons that will always return a zero or always return strictly positive numbers for all training data points.
comments.explanation: The definition provided is binary: 'always zero' or 'always strictly positive'. However, for a ReLU neuron, 'always strictly positive' does not capture the distinction between a neuron that is 'always active' (functioning as an affine transformation) and one that might change its state. The current text conflates these states, potentially misidentifying the necessary condition for the 'affine' replacement step applied in the pruning algorithm.

comments.title: Underspecified cluster number selection
comments.quote: It is up to us to determine the number of clusters, and hence the final number of neurons after reduction
comments.explanation: The algorithm proposes a data-dependent compression but fails to provide criteria (e.g., silhouette score, variance threshold, or error tolerance) for choosing the number of clusters $k$. Leaving this hyperparameter entirely to the user without a heuristic makes the method underspecified and complicates reproducibility.

comments.title: Unjustified claim regarding extrapolation behavior
comments.quote: In fact, it will be different outside of the convex hull of the training data. But these changes further smoothen the extrapolation behavior
comments.explanation: The authors define the functional change outside the convex hull as 'smoothening,' which is subjective and lacks formal justification. Since ReLU network extrapolation is governed by the learned weights and architecture, labeling this modification an improvement to the extrapolation behavior without formalizing how the inductive bias is controlled is speculative.

comments.title: Theoretical inconsistency in generalization bound claims
comments.quote: Worth noting is that if one tries to evaluate an upper bound of the generalization gap... one could potentially find a better estimation by taking the sum of the weights after reduction.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest that post-reduction weight sums yield better generalization bounds. Generalization bounds typically depend on the complexity of the learned function rather than the parameterization of the network. The authors fail to explain how a post-processing reduction step influences the relevant functional norm or why it should improve the estimation without a formal connection to regularization theory.

comments.title: Architectural contradiction regarding network topology
comments.quote: Also of interest is that, after the reduction algorithm was applied, we obtain a fully-connected neural network.
comments.explanation: This statement contradicts earlier claims (Remark 2.1) that the algorithm produces networks with skip connections. The text is ambiguous as to whether the resulting architecture is truly fully-connected or if the skip connections introduced by the algorithm are being overlooked, creating confusion for the reader regarding the final network structure.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and potentially high-impact problem: compressing over-parameterized ReLU networks by "condensing" neurons rather than relying on traditional pruning, which avoids the need for extensive retraining. The motivation is clear, and the connection to $L_1$-type P-functionals provides a robust mathematical foundation that distinguishes this work from heuristic pruning methods. However, the submission currently suffers from significant clarity and structural issues. The reliance on "unpublished work of the authors" to justify key theoretical claims significantly weakens the paper's self-contained nature and credibility, and the transition from the problem statement to the proposed "Deep Stack Network" architecture feels abrupt. The manuscript would benefit from a more rigorous formalization of the reduction algorithm itself—which is currently only vaguely described—as well as a clearer distinction between the specific architecture proposed and standard deep learning models. As it stands, the paper feels like a preliminary report rather than a complete, rigorous submission suitable for a top-tier venue, as critical mathematical definitions and implementation details are missing.

comments.title: Possible OCR-induced notation error in dimension definition
comments.quote: V^{(j)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_j \times d_{j-1}} \text{ and } b^{(j)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_j}
comments.explanation: The text states $V^{(j)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_j \times d_{j-1}}$. In the architecture described, $d_j$ is the dimension of the bottleneck (output) of the stack, and $d_{j-1}$ is the dimension of the previous bottleneck (input). However, the OCR transcript renders this as $V^{(j)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_j \times d_j-1}$. While this is likely an OCR artifact for $d_{j-1}$, a careless reader might interpret it as $(d_j) - 1$, which would be a dimension mismatch if $d_j$ is a vector size and not a scalar index. Given the context of deep stack networks, $d_{j-1}$ is clearly intended, but the notation is currently ambiguous/misleading due to the formatting.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'outside neurons'
comments.quote: We define the outside neurons as those neurons that will always return a zero or always return strictly positive numbers for all training data points.
comments.explanation: The definition provided is binary: 'always zero' or 'always strictly positive'. However, for a ReLU neuron $\max(0, v^T x + b)$, 'always positive' is insufficient to characterize the behavior for pruning or shifting to affine weights; one needs to know if the neuron is 'always active' (i.e., $v^T x + b > 0$ for all $x$ in the training set). If the neuron activation is always positive, its output is $v^T x + b$. If it is always zero, its output is 0. The current text conflates 'always return strictly positive' with 'acting as an identity/affine function', which is only true if the neuron is *never* deactivated (always in the linear regime of the ReLU). The prose is technically loose, potentially misidentifying the condition required for the 'affine' replacement step.

comments.title: Underspecified cluster number selection
comments.quote: It is up to us to determine the number of clusters, and hence the final number of neurons after reduction
comments.explanation: The algorithm essentially proposes a data-dependent compression. While the authors state it is 'up to us', they provide no criteria (e.g., silhouette score, variance threshold, or error tolerance) for choosing the number of clusters $k$. Since the paper claims to perform autonomous reduction, leaving this hyperparameter entirely to the user without a heuristic or strategy for selection makes the method underspecified and difficult to reproduce.

comments.title: Claim of 'no change' in function vs extrapolation behavior
comments.quote: In fact, it will be different outside of the convex hull of the training data. But these changes further smoothen the extrapolation behavior
comments.explanation: The authors claim there is 'provably almost no change of the learned function' while admitting it changes outside the convex hull. In ReLU networks, the behavior outside the convex hull determines the global extrapolation, which is often a major component of the function's learned properties. Describing this as 'smoothening' rather than a modification of the model's inductive bias is a subjective, potentially questionable claim that lacks formal justification regarding how the extrapolation is constrained or controlled by the reduction.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in claim regarding generalization bounds
comments.quote: Worth noting is that if one tries to evaluate an upper bound of the generalization gap (difference between the empirical error and true error) based on the sum of the weights, (Kawaguchi et al., 2017), one could potentially find a better estimation by taking the sum of the weights after reduction.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest that using the 'sum of the weights' (presumably referring to the L1 norm of weights or related complexity measures) of the reduced network provides a better generalization gap bound. However, generalization bounds (like those discussed in Kawaguchi et al., 2017) are typically dependent on the complexity of the function class or the norms applied to the *original* architecture's parameter space. Reducing the network (the algorithm) is a post-processing step; the generalization guarantee depends on the complexity of the *function learned*, not the specific neural realization. To claim 'better estimation' for the same function, one must argue that the reduction process preserves the relevant functional norm while potentially reducing the 'sum of weights' calculation if that sum is parameter-count dependent. The claim is overly speculative and lacks a link to the regularization theory developed earlier in the paper.

comments.title: Under-specified claim about the output architecture
comments.quote: Also of interest is that, after the reduction algorithm was applied, we obtain a fully-connected neural network.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper (Remark 2.1), the authors state that the algorithm 'could also be applied to an ordinary fully connected deep ReLU neural network and would output a neural network with skip connections.' The section now claims that applying the reduction algorithm results in a 'fully-connected' network. This is ambiguous: does it mean the skip connections (added by the algorithm) are being ignored, or that the topology remains a specific variation of a fully-connected network? Given the emphasis on 'stacks' and 'bottlenecks' which often imply non-standard connectivity (like skip connections), this statement contradicts the structural description given in Section 2.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: As an expert reviewer, I find that the paper addresses an interesting and practically relevant problem—reducing the number of neurons in deep ReLU networks while preserving the learned function—and attempts to ground its approach in the theory of implicit and explicit regularization. However, the manuscript suffers from several significant issues that undermine its quality and clarity. The most critical problem is the heavy reliance on unpublished work by the authors themselves (repeatedly cited as "unpublished" or "the authors have shown in an unpublished paper"), which makes it impossible to assess the theoretical foundations, the claimed equivalences, or the justification of the algorithm without access to those results—this is unacceptable for a conference submission and raises questions about the completeness and reproducibility of the work. Moreover, the architecture description (the "One Stack Network" and "Deep Stack Network") is presented in a dense, notation-heavy manner with insufficient motivation for the design choices; the introduction of affine layers, skip connections, and bottlenecks feels ad hoc, and the rationale for why this specific structure enables theoretically-grounded reduction is not clearly explained within the provided text. The abstract’s claim of "provably almost no change of the learned function" is not supported by any proof or rigorous argument in the excerpt, and the experimental evidence is merely alluded to without any quantitative results or discussion of datasets, baselines, or failure cases. Overall, while the topic is promising, the paper lacks self-contained theoretical justification, clarity of exposition, and empirical validation in the presented portion, making it unsuitable for acceptance in its current form.

comments.title: Citation format missing parentheses
comments.quote: (Maennel et al, 2018)
comments.explanation: The citation '(Maennel et al, 2018)' is missing parentheses around the year and uses a comma instead of a semicolon or standard formatting. While this is a minor formatting issue, it could cause confusion in a formal academic paper, where standard citation style (e.g., (Maennel et al., 2018)) is expected.

comments.title: Unpublished work referenced as authoritative
comments.quote: unpublished work of the authors on deep neural networks (with arbitrarily many inputs and outputs).
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly references an 'unpublished paper by the authors' as the basis for the theoretical P-functional characterization (e.g., in Section 3 and elsewhere). The passage says 'the authors have shown in an unpublished paper that the solution of the optimization problem can be characterized...' This undermines the reproducibility and verifiability of the theoretical claims, since the core theoretical foundation is not publicly available. A reader cannot evaluate or validate the claimed equivalence between weight regularization and P-functionals without this unpublished work. This is a moderate issue because the paper does provide some citations to published works (e.g., Neyshabur et al., Ongie et al., Savarese et al.) that partly support the theory, but the specific deep-stack extension relies on unpublished results.

comments.title: Unclear weighting function in P-functional formula
comments.explanation: The OCR renders the weighting function g(r) as '~~_√_~~ r 1[2] +1[' which is garbled. From context (footnote 3, page 4), the intended form is presumably g(r) = 1/√(r^2 + 1) or similar, but the text is corrupted. The surrounding discussion indicates that the weighting function handles boundary conditions and affects the regularization. The garbled equation makes it impossible for a reader to understand the exact form of the P-functional, which is central to the theoretical justification. This is a moderate issue because the general concept (L1-like norm) is still communicated, but the specific formula is lost.

comments.title: Potential index error in V dimension specification
comments.explanation: The dimension of V^{(j)} is written as n_j × d_{j-1}. However, earlier in the passage (page 2), the input to the stack is described as having dimension d_{j-1} (the previous bottleneck). This seems consistent. However, the notation uses 'd_{j-1}' with a subscript j-1, which is plausible but could be an indexing error if the first stack's input dimension is d_0 (the original input dimension). If the paper previously defined bottlenecks as d_j, then d_{j-1} is correct. The context does not show the initial definition of d_0, but it is a standard convention. This is not a clear error, so I flag it only as a minor potential ambiguity.

comments.title: Overclaim about 'provably almost no change' on training data
comments.quote: We discuss two experiments which illustrate the efficiency of the algorithm to reduce the number of neurons significantly with provably almost no change of the learned function within the training data
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that the algorithm provides 'provably almost no change of the learned function within the training data'. However, in the Reduction Algorithm section (4.1), the paper notes that removing outside neurons does not change the function on the training data, but then in Section 4.2 (removing weakest neurons) and 4.3 (clustering), there is no formal proof that the function remains unchanged on the training data. The experiments show numerical results, but the claim of 'provably' is not substantiated beyond the first step. This overstates what is actually shown. The clustering step in particular introduces approximations via k-means, which will change the function. Therefore, the 'almost no loss in accuracy' is empirically observed, not proven for all steps.

comments.title: Ambiguity in clustering step: unweighted vs weighted k-means and formula for new v, b, w
comments.quote: We first group the neurons according to their kink-representation, using (weighted) k-means clustering. It is up to us to determine the number of clusters, and hence the final number of neurons after reduction, because for every cluster we will define one neuron summarizing all the information. The kink-representation of every neuron is computed as following: a concatenation of _∥[b] v[k] k[ v] ∥[k]_[2] 2[and] _∥vvkk∥_ 2[,] and are weighted according to: _∥vk∥_ 2 _∥wk∥_ 2.
comments.explanation: The description of the clustering step is underspecified. It says the kink-representation is a concatenation of b_k/∥v_k∥_2 and ∥v_k∥_2, but the exact vector used for clustering is not fully clear. The weighting factor (∥v_k∥_2 ∥w_k∥_2) is mentioned, but whether it is used in the k-means distance calculation or for feature weighting is ambiguous. The subsequent formulas for new v, b, and w (not fully shown in the passage) are referenced but not provided clearly. This lack of detail makes it difficult for a reader to reproduce the algorithm, especially the clustering step. This is a moderate issue because the overall algorithm outline is present, but the clustering details are critical for reproduction.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'outside neuron' detection
comments.quote: We decided to loop through every ReLU neuron, then check their prediction for every training data point. If the prediction was always zero or always strictly positive, we could be sure that we found an outside neuron. Of course such a method is also computationally expensive, and would hence be very impractical if we had many neurons and many training data points. But we observed empirically that it was not necessary to loop through every training data point (more on this in the experimentation chapter).
comments.explanation: The detection of 'outside neurons' is described as checking every training data point for each neuron, which is O(n_neurons * n_data). The paper then says it was not necessary to loop through every training data point, but does not specify what approximation or heuristic was actually used. The claim that this detection is 'practical' without further detail leaves the method underspecified. A reader cannot reproduce the procedure without knowing the sampling strategy or alternative criterion. This is a minor-to-moderate issue because the experimentation chapter might provide details, but within the provided context, the method is vague.

comments.title: Singular Value Decomposition on W_affine is not fully explained
comments.quote: Singular Value Decomposition is going to be applied to W_affine and we obtain three matrices U, Σ and V, which allows to update the affine weights by adding U ~~_√_~~ Σ to the affine weights between the input and the affine layer, and by adding ~~_√_~~ Σ V to the affine weights between the affine and the output (or bottleneck!) layer.
comments.explanation: The SVD update for the affine weights is described briefly, but it is unclear how this step preserves the learned function. The text says to add U√Σ and √Σ V to the affine weights, but the original affine weights are also present (since the outside neurons' contributions were added). The combination of existing affine weights and the SVD factor is not clearly justified mathematically. Additionally, the effect of this decomposition on the represented function is not explained. The reader is left wondering whether this step changes the output or is simply a reparameterization. This is a moderate issue because it undermines the claim that the function remains almost unchanged.

comments.title: Missing equation for single wide shallow network P-functional
comments.quote:
For a single wide shallow neural network we get[2]

handles the boundary condition, S^{d-1} denoted the (d-1)-dimensional unit sphere and we have a weighing function[3] g(r) = ...
comments.explanation: The sentence states that for a single wide shallow network we obtain a certain P-functional, but the actual equation is not given. Instead, the next line begins describing the boundary condition and the sphere. This missing formula is critical because the paper later relies on the form of this functional (specifically the presence of ∥·∥_2 instead of ∥·∥_2^2) to claim that the optimal function can be represented by a finite number of neurons. Without the equation, the reader cannot verify this central claim. The missing formula may be due to OCR loss, but it leaves a gap in the argument.

comments.title: Garbled quantity for removing weakest neurons
comments.quote: This can be done by calculating the quantity  _∥vk∥_[2] 2[+] _[ b]_[2] _k[∥][w][k][∥]_ 2[for each neuron] _[ k]_[.
comments.explanation: The formula for the quantity used to prune the weakest neurons is corrupted by OCR. It likely intends something like ∥v_k∥_2^2 + b_k^2 ∥w_k∥_2, but the exponent placement and the multiplication by ∥w_k∥_2 are ambiguous. This ambiguity could lead to an incorrect implementation of the pruning step. The authors should provide a clear, unambiguous formula.

comments.title: Uninterpretable kink-representation for clustering
comments.quote: The kink-representation of every neuron is computed as following: a concatenation of ∥[b] v[k] k[ v] ∥[k]_[2] 2[and] ∥vvkk∥_2[,] and are weighted according to: ∥vk∥_2 ∥wk∥_2.
comments.explanation: The description of how to construct the kink-representation for each neuron is severely garbled beyond recognition. It is impossible to determine whether the representation is [b_k/∥v_k∥_2, ∥v_k∥_2] or some other vector. The weighting factor (∥v_k∥_2 ∥w_k∥_2) is clear, but the representation itself is essential for the k-means clustering step. This part of the algorithm cannot be reproduced from the given text.

comments.title: Missing formulas for new kink position and strength in clustering step
comments.quote:
First, the new kink position will be determined according to this formula:

We define the strength:

Finally we can define the new _v_ , _b_ , and _w_ :
comments.explanation: After stating that the new kink position will be determined by a formula, and that a strength is defined, no actual formulas are provided. The core of the clustering step (how to compute the combined neuron’s parameters from a cluster) remains unspecified. While some details might be inferred from the surrounding text, the lack of explicit equations prevents exact reproduction of the algorithm.

comments.title: Garbled formula for g(ξ) due to OCR artifacts
comments.quote: With _g_ ( _ξ_ ) = ~~_√_~~ _ξ_ 1[2] +1[and] _[ k]_[the index of the neurons in a selected cluster.]
comments.explanation: The formula for g(ξ) is corrupted by OCR. The intended expression is g(ξ) = √(ξ² + 1), as used earlier in the paper (see the summary of key equations). The rendering with tildes and the exponent written as '1[2]' is misleading and could cause confusion, especially if a reader misinterprets the exponent as 12 rather than squared. While the intended symbol is clear from context, the notational error is distracting and could affect reproducibility if a reader tries to implement the clustering formula exactly as printed.

comments.title: Underspecified method for detecting outside neurons
comments.quote: We decided to loop through every ReLU neuron, then check their prediction for every training data point. ... But we observed empirically that it was not necessary to loop through every training data point (more on this in the experimentation chapter).
comments.explanation: The authors describe a straightforward but computationally expensive procedure to identify outside neurons (checking every neuron against every training point). They then note that they found a way to avoid this expense, promising details in the experimentation chapter. However, the 'Experiments' section (Section 5) provides no further information on the practical shortcut used. Without this detail, a reader cannot reproduce the algorithm without either re-deriving the shortcut or incurring the heavy computational cost. This constitutes an underspecified method that undermines reproducibility.

comments.title: Mischaracterisation of reduced network as 'fully-connected'
comments.quote: Also of interest is that, after the reduction algorithm was applied, we obtain a fully-connected neural network.
comments.explanation: The architecture described throughout the paper (Figures 3–5) includes skip connections via affine layers (linear activation, no bias). The reduction algorithm (Section 4) removes outside neurons, prunes weak neurons, and clusters the rest, but it never removes the affine weights; in fact, Section 4.1 explicitly adds contributions to the affine weights and updates them via SVD. Therefore the resulting network still has two parallel paths per stack: the main ReLU-to-bottleneck path and the skip path through the affine layer. Calling this a 'fully-connected neural network' (which conventionally implies a single feedforward path without skip connections) is misleading and contradicts the paper's own architectural description. A reader could incorrectly infer that the reduction collapses the architecture into a plain feedforward network, which would affect understanding of subsequent compression possibilities.

comments.title: Unsupported speculation about generalization gap bound
comments.quote: Worth noting is that if one tries to evaluate an upper bound of the generalization gap (difference between the empirical error and true error) based on the sum of the weights, (Kawaguchi et al., 2017), one could potentially find a better estimation by taking the sum of the weights after reduction.
comments.explanation: The paper does not derive any generalization bound, nor does it show that the 'sum of the weights' (which is not precisely defined—presumably the ℓ₁ norm or total magnitude) after reduction yields a tighter bound. The claim is presented as a matter-of-fact observation, but it is entirely speculative and unsupported by any analysis or experiment in the paper. While it appears in a concluding/future-work paragraph, the phrasing 'Worth noting is that… one could potentially find a better estimation' overstates what has been demonstrated. A careful reader would expect at least a sketch of why the sum of weights after reduction would produce a better estimate (e.g., because weights are condensed or because the reduced network has fewer parameters with different norms). The connection to Kawaguchi et al. (2017) is also vague: that paper studies generalization bounds in terms of path-norms, not simply the sum of weights, so the reference is imprecise.

comments.title: Undefined 'sum of the weights' with ambiguous reference
comments.quote: based on the sum of the weights, (Kawaguchi et al., 2017)
comments.explanation: The phrase 'sum of the weights' is ambiguous—it could mean the sum of absolute values, the sum of squares, or the total number of non-zero weights. Kawaguchi et al. (2017) discuss generalization bounds using path-norms and other norms, not a simple unweighted sum. The paper does not define which metric is intended, and no equation or derivation is provided. Since the claimed benefit of the reduction algorithm (better estimation) hinges on this quantity, the lack of clarity makes the statement hard to interpret or verify. However, because this is a future-work remark and not central to the paper's contributions, the severity is minor.

This is a moderate issue because it undermines the claim that the function remains almost unchanged.

comments.title: Missing equation for single wide shallow network P-functional
comments.quote:
For a single wide shallow neural network we get[2]

handles the boundary condition, S^{d-1} denoted the (d-1)-dimensional unit sphere and we have a weighing function[3] g(r) = ...
comments.explanation: The sentence states that for a single wide shallow network we obtain a certain P-functional, but the actual equation is not given. Instead, the next line begins describing the boundary condition and the sphere. This missing formula is critical because the paper later relies on the form of this functional (specifically the presence of ∥·∥_2 instead of ∥·∥_2^2) to claim that the optimal function can be represented by a finite number of neurons. Without the equation, the reader cannot verify this central claim. The missing formula may be due to OCR loss, but it leaves a gap in the argument.

comments.explanation: The formula for the quantity used to prune the weakest neurons is corrupted by OCR. It likely intends something like ∥v_k∥_2^2 + b_k^2 ∥w_k∥_2, but the exponent placement and the multiplication by ∥w_k∥_2 are ambiguous. This ambiguity could lead to an incorrect implementation of the pruning step. The authors should provide a clear, unambiguous formula.

comments.title: Uninterpretable kink-representation for clustering
comments.quote: The kink-representation of every neuron is computed as following: a concatenation of ∥[b] v[k] k[ v] ∥[k]_[2] 2[and] ∥vvkk∥_2[,] and are weighted according to: ∥vk∥_2 ∥wk∥_2.
comments.explanation: The description of how to construct the kink-representation for each neuron is severely garbled beyond recognition. It is impossible to determine whether the representation is [b_k/∥v_k∥_2, ∥v_k∥_2] or some other vector. The weighting factor (∥v_k∥_2 ∥w_k∥_2) is clear, but the representation itself is essential for the k-means clustering step. This part of the algorithm cannot be reproduced from the given text.

comments.quote:
First, the new kink position will be determined according to this formula:

We define the strength:

Finally we can define the new _v_ , _b_ , and _w_ :
comments.explanation: After stating that the new kink position will be determined by a formula, and that a strength is defined, no actual formulas are provided. The core of the clustering step (how to compute the combined neuron’s parameters from a cluster) remains unspecified. While some details might be inferred from the surrounding text, the lack of explicit equations prevents exact reproduction of the algorithm.

comments.title: Garbled formula for g(ξ) due to OCR artifacts
comments.quote: With _g_ ( _ξ_ ) = ~~_√_~~ _ξ_ 1[2] +1[and] _[ k]_[the index of the neurons in a selected cluster.]
comments.explanation: The formula for g(ξ) is corrupted by OCR. The intended expression is g(ξ) = √(ξ² + 1), as used earlier in the paper (see the summary of key equations). The rendering with tildes and the exponent written as '1[2]' is misleading and could cause confusion, especially if a reader misinterprets the exponent as 12 rather than squared. While the intended symbol is clear from context, the notational error is distracting and could affect reproducibility if a reader tries to implement the clustering formula exactly as printed.

comments.title: Underspecified method for detecting outside neurons
comments.quote: We decided to loop through every ReLU neuron, then check their prediction for every training data point. ... But we observed empirically that it was not necessary to loop through every training data point (more on this in the experimentation chapter).
comments.explanation: The authors describe a straightforward but computationally expensive procedure to identify outside neurons (checking every neuron against every training point). They then note that they found a way to avoid this expense, promising details in the experimentation chapter. However, the 'Experiments' section (Section 5) provides no further information on the practical shortcut used. Without this detail, a reader cannot reproduce the algorithm without either re-deriving the shortcut or incurring the heavy computational cost. This constitutes an underspecified method that undermines reproducibility.

comments.title: Mischaracterisation of reduced network as 'fully-connected'
comments.quote: Also of interest is that, after the reduction algorithm was applied, we obtain a fully-connected neural network.
comments.explanation: The architecture described throughout the paper (Figures 3–5) includes skip connections via affine layers (linear activation, no bias). The reduction algorithm (Section 4) removes outside neurons, prunes weak neurons, and clusters the rest, but it never removes the affine weights; in fact, Section 4.1 explicitly adds contributions to the affine weights and updates them via SVD. Therefore the resulting network still has two parallel paths per stack: the main ReLU-to-bottleneck path and the skip path through the affine layer. Calling this a 'fully-connected neural network' (which conventionally implies a single feedforward path without skip connections) is misleading and contradicts the paper's own architectural description. A reader could incorrectly infer that the reduction collapses the architecture into a plain feedforward network, which would affect understanding of subsequent compression possibilities.

comments.title: Unsupported speculation about generalization gap bound
comments.quote: Worth noting is that if one tries to evaluate an upper bound of the generalization gap (difference between the empirical error and true error) based on the sum of the weights, (Kawaguchi et al., 2017), one could potentially find a better estimation by taking the sum of the weights after reduction.
comments.explanation: The paper does not derive any generalization bound, nor does it show that the 'sum of the weights' (which is not precisely defined—presumably the ℓ₁ norm or total magnitude) after reduction yields a tighter bound. The claim is presented as a matter-of-fact observation, but it is entirely speculative and unsupported by any analysis or experiment in the paper. While it appears in a concluding/future-work paragraph, the phrasing 'Worth noting is that… one could potentially find a better estimation' overstates what has been demonstrated. A careful reader would expect at least a sketch of why the sum of weights after reduction would produce a better estimate (e.g., because weights are condensed or because the reduced network has fewer parameters with different norms). The connection to Kawaguchi et al. (2017) is also vague: that paper studies generalization bounds in terms of path-norms, not simply the sum of weights, so the reference is imprecise.

comments.title: Undefined 'sum of the weights' with ambiguous reference
comments.quote: based on the sum of the weights, (Kawaguchi et al., 2017)
comments.explanation: The phrase 'sum of the weights' is ambiguous—it could mean the sum of absolute values, the sum of squares, or the total number of non-zero weights. Kawaguchi et al. (2017) discuss generalization bounds using path-norms and other norms, not a simple unweighted sum. The paper does not define which metric is intended, and no equation or derivation is provided. Since the claimed benefit of the reduction algorithm (better estimation) hinges on this quantity, the lack of clarity makes the statement hard to interpret or verify. However, because this is a future-work remark and not central to the paper's contributions, the severity is minor.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript presents a Reduction Algorithm for Deep ReLU networks grounded in P-functional regularization theory, claiming the ability to compress trained models by 90–99% with negligible accuracy loss and without retraining. While the theoretical motivation connecting weight regularization to finite neuron representations is compelling, the paper currently lacks the rigor and clarity required for acceptance. The most significant issue is the misalignment between the abstract's broad promise of applicability to general Deep ReLU Networks and the method's dependence on a highly specific "Deep Stack Network" architecture featuring linear bottlenecks and infinite-width assumptions; the authors must demonstrate that their condensation mechanism generalizes beyond this constrained topology to standard MLPs and CNNs, rather than being limited to the proposed structure. Additionally, the theoretical framework relies heavily on "unpublished work of the authors," which impedes reproducibility and independent verification, necessitating the inclusion of essential derivations or citations to published literature to substantiate the algorithm's foundations. Finally, the narrative regarding retraining is inconsistent: the abstract and introduction emphasize a retraining-free reduction, yet Section 1.2 acknowledges that iterative retraining improves results, suggesting the "no retraining" claim may be overstated or requires careful qualification regarding the trade-off between immediate deployability and final performance. Major revisions are required to clarify the method's scope, address the gap between infinite-width theory and finite practice, and provide empirical validation on standard benchmarks to verify the reduction efficacy.

comments.title: Ambiguous architectural description post-reduction
comments.quote: Also of interest is that, after the reduction algorithm was applied, we obtain a fully-connected neural network.
comments.explanation: Initially, this statement raised a slight flag because it appears to contradict the explicit architecture defined earlier in the paper. In Section 2 and Figures 4–5, the authors formally define a 'Deep Stack Network' consisting of wide ReLU layers interleaved with finite-dimensional 'bottleneck' layers ($d_j \ll n_j$). I checked the reduction algorithm in Section 4 and Remark 4.5 to see if the bottlenecks are removed or merged. The algorithm explicitly condenses ReLU neurons, prunes outside neurons into affine skip-connection weights, and uses SVD to redistribute affine contributions, but it leaves the bottleneck layers structurally intact. I considered whether 'fully-connected' was being used informally to mean 'dense' (i.e., non-sparse weight matrices, which aligns with their Section 1.1 claim that the method 'does not introduce sparsity'). While that contextual reading is plausible, standard deep learning terminology reserves 'fully-connected' for layer topology (where every neuron in layer $l$ connects to every neuron in $l+1$ without dimensionality reduction), not weight density. Because the reduced model explicitly retains the bottlenecked, stacked topology, labeling it 'fully-connected' is technically imprecise and could mislead readers about the final architecture or the implications of applying subsequent pruning. Clarifying that the reduced networks contain dense (non-sparse) layers within a preserved bottlenecked stack structure would resolve this ambiguity.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel method for reducing the number of neurons in deep ReLU networks by reformulating the architecture around "One Stack Networks" and leveraging recent regularization theory to justify the compression of wide layers into smaller bottlenecks. The motivation and differentiation from standard pruning techniques (such as Lottery Tickets or magnitude-based pruning) are clear, positioning the work as a theoretically grounded approach to "condensation" rather than removal. However, the submission currently suffers from significant clarity and completeness issues. The reliance on ASCII art to describe figures instead of standard captions, combined with the abrupt cutoff of the theoretical derivation in the provided excerpt, undermines the formal academic presentation expected at ICLR. To be accepted, the authors must address these formatting flaws and complete the mathematical justification, as the current draft lacks the necessary rigor to convincingly prove that function accuracy is preserved during compression.
